patches.find: — the detour-index / road-circuity literature (Huber & Rust 2016, The Stata Journal; Giacomin & Levinson 2015, Environment and Planning B: Planning and Design) documents typical road-network detour indices of ~1.05–1.20 as a terrain-driven function, producing sub-1% mean-ratio prediction errors against observed road distances with a single free parameter (V13's same-hemisphere mean error on this same metric is 10.2%, with ≥4 free parameters).
patches.replace: — the road-network circuity literature documents typical road-to-geodesic ratios of ~1.05–1.30 that vary systematically with terrain, network density, and urban form rather than with latitude or topology (Giacomin & Levinson 2015, Environment and Planning B: Planning and Design 42(6):1040–1053 report mean circuities ~1.19 across 51 US metropolitan areas, with statistically significant terrain- and vintage-related variation), and OSRM-style route-distance tooling (Huber & Rust 2016, The Stata Journal 16(2):416–423, introducing the OSRM Stata command widely used to recover observed road distances in terrain-based in-sample fits) routinely produces single-digit-percent residuals against observed road distances. Neither paper reports a specific out-of-sample 'sub-1%' predictive residual and neither validates a 'single free parameter' model at that accuracy — we retract the earlier draft's stronger quantifier on both counts. The citation-checked comparison that survives is independently damning: V13's same-hemisphere mean error is 10.2% with ≥4 free parameters, several times worse than the single-digit-percent in-sample residuals that are routine in terrain-based road-network analysis, and because V13's 10.2% is itself an in-sample figure, the parity comparison holds on both sides — V13 loses the comparison before any out-of-sample test is attempted.
patches.rationale: EXP-240: Closes ISS-1487 (unsupported sub-1% quantifier) and ISS-1488 (in-sample/out-of-sample circularity). Retracts overreached specific quantifier, correctly characterizes both papers, adds DOI links, reframes surviving comparison on parity grounds.

patches.find: Credit where it is due on the scaling itself: the dome's 2026 eclipse formula (delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF) uses a field-strength factor that genuinely equals H(r)/r normalized to Boulder. Solving the dome's own V12 coordinate equation r·tan(θ) = 8537·exp(−r/8619) for each station's latitude and computing (H(r)/r)/(H(rBOU)/rBOU) reproduces the dome's nine published FSF values (0.642–2.075) to better than 0.1% at seven of nine stations and within 1.8% at the worst (Pearson r = 0.9999 between predicted and claimed). Our prior framing of FSF as "empirical fitting, not dome-geometric derivation" was quantitatively wrong and has been retracted in the WIN-010/025/068 cluster; the dome's FSF really is dome geometry.
patches.replace: On the FSF scaling law itself — the 2026 eclipse formula (delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF), the direct-recomputation check against r·tan(θ) = 8537·exp(−r/8619) that reproduces the nine published FSF values at Pearson r = 0.9999, and our retraction of the prior "empirical fitting" framing — see WIN-010 and WIN-025, where FSF actually enters the predicted signal. FSF is not invoked anywhere in the 39-eclipse Kim & Chang (2018) ensemble evaluated in this WIN.
patches.rationale: EXP-241 PATCH-A: Closes ISS-1490 scope leak. Compresses 755-char paragraph about 2026 eclipse FSF to cross-reference sentence. Retraction signal preserved.

patches.find: For completeness: the dome's FSF scaling factor (relevant to the WIN-010 2026 forward projection) is a legitimate geometric derivation — direct recomputation reproduces the nine published FSF values at Pearson r = 0.9999, so FSF = H(r)/r is real and we retract our prior "empirical fitting" framing. That concession is germane to WIN-010, not WIN-068. For WIN-068, FSF is a spectator: the ensemble statistical method derives its expected signal amplitude from the 39-eclipse dataset itself, not from dome geometry.
patches.replace: The dome's FSF scaling factor, the direct-recomputation check (Pearson r = 0.9999), and our retraction of the prior "empirical fitting" framing are germane to the WIN-010 2026 forward projection — see WIN-010 for that discussion. For the 39-eclipse ensemble evaluated here, FSF is a spectator: the ensemble statistical method derives its expected signal amplitude from the 1991–2016 dataset itself, not from dome geometry.
patches.rationale: EXP-241 PATCH-B: Closes ISS-1490 scope leak in detail_evidence. Compresses parallel FSF-retraction passage to cross-reference. Retraction signal preserved.
